text: The surgeon begins by freeing adhesions between the omentum and the small bowel on the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, followed by initiating a medial to lateral dissection.
The surgeon begins by freeing adhesions between the omentum and the small bowel on the medial aspect of the sigmoid colon, followed by a medial to lateral dissection.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, preserving the retroperitoneal plane, and proceeds with a high ligation of the superior hemorrhoidal artery or the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) to optimize bowel length.

The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery, carefully skeletonizing the vessel to ensure a clean division. The ureter is checked for proximity before using a vessel sealer to divide the artery, ensuring no surrounding tissue impedes the seal.

The surgeon isolates the superior hemorrhoidal artery, carefully skeletonizing the vessel to ensure a clean division. The ureter is checked for proximity before using the vessel sealer to divide the artery, ensuring no surrounding tissue impedes the seal.

The surgeon identifies the tethering of the left colic artery and plans to continue the medial dissection. A high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMB) is performed, with the intention to release tension by taking the left colic artery.

The surgeon identifies the left colic vessel, noting its tethering effect, and plans to continue the medial dissection. A high ligation of the inferior mesenteric vein (IMB) is performed, with the intention to take the left colic vessel to release tension before mobilizing the splenic flexure.
The surgeon isolates the left colic branch and prepares to divide it using a vessel sealer, opting not to perform a high ligation of the inferior mesenteric artery (IMA) in this instance.

The surgeon isolates the left colic branch and uses a vessel sealer to divide it. The medial to lateral dissection continues up to the splenic flexure for maximal mobilization. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is also isolated and divided using the vessel sealer.

The surgeon performs a medial to lateral dissection, progressing towards the splenic flexure for maximal mobilization. The inferior mesenteric vein (IMV) is isolated and divided using a vessel sealer.

The surgeon completes the full medial dissection and begins the lateral dissection, placing a sponge to protect the ureter. The entry into the lateral plane is facilitated by the extensive medial dissection.

The surgeon completes the medial dissection and begins the lateral dissection, placing a sponge to protect the ureter. The entry into the lateral plane is facilitated by the prior medial dissection, and the surgeon progresses towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon approaches from the lateral side, revealing the medial plane for entry. The dissection progresses upwards towards the splenic flexure.
The surgeon exposes the kidney and the tail of the pancreas, then transitions to the pelvic region to continue freeing up the remaining diseased tissue.

The surgeon exposes the kidney and the tail of the pancreas, revealing these anatomical structures clearly. The focus then shifts back to the pelvis to continue freeing up the remaining diseased tissue.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection on the left side wall, carefully identifying anatomical planes to avoid encroaching upon the ureter, which is clearly visualized.
The surgeon completes the lateral dissection on the left side wall, carefully identifying anatomical planes to avoid encroaching upon the ureter, which is clearly visible.

The surgeon divides the margins, starting with the upper rectum, and proceeds to take the mesentery to the proximal margin. The previously mentioned diverticulum is visible, and the surgeon approaches from beneath the ligated superior hemorrhoidal artery.
The surgeon prepares to divide the margins, focusing on the upper rectum. The mesentery is then taken to the proximal margin, ensuring clear separation of these structures.
The surgeon identifies the diverticulum located proximal to the ligated superior hemorrhoidal artery, visible in the distance near the grasper, preparing for its excision.
The surgeon identifies a diverticulum located proximal to the grasper, preparing to excise it. A perfusion assessment is performed before proceeding with the division.

The surgeon completes the division of the bowel and creates an anastomosis using absorbable V-lock sutures, joining the outer posterior row by aligning the staple lines. The specimen is removed via a Pfannenstiel incision and placed in the right upper quadrant.

The surgeon places the specimen in the right upper quadrant and begins creating an anastomosis. A hand-sewn technique is used with absorbable V-lock sutures, starting with the outer posterior row by joining the two staple lines. The surgeon then performs a partotomy followed by a collotomy.

The surgeon performs a partotomy followed by a collotomy, preparing to sew the inner row of the anastomosis using a baseball stitch technique on the posterior lip, maneuvering around the far corner of the surgical site.

The surgeon performs a hand-sewn anastomosis using absorbable V-lock sutures, sewing the inner row in a baseball stitch pattern on the posterior lip. The transition stitch is applied to position the suture on the outside, starting on the posterior row inside on the near side and sewing out and around. The anterior row is imbricated by canalizing over the top.

The surgeon performs a transition stitch to position the suture on the outside, beginning with the posterior row on the near side. The stitch is sewn out and around, followed by canalization over the top to imbricate the anterior row.

The surgeon alternates between two sutures to ensure the closure of the anterior wall, then returns to the initial suture to imbricate the anterior suture line over the top of the back row of staple lines.

The surgeon alternates between two sutures to ensure closure of the anterior wall, then uses the initial suture to imbricate the anterior suture line over the staple lines. A leak test is performed using a rigid proctoscopy scope or flexible sigmoidoscopy, confirming a widely patent anastomosis with no bleeding or leaks.
The surgeon performs a leak test on the anastomosis using a 19mm rigid proctoscopy scope or flexible sigmoidoscopy, observing that the anastomosis is widely patent with no bleeding and no leakage.
The surgical specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision at the conclusion of the colectomy procedure.
The specimen is extracted through a Pfannenstiel incision at the end of the surgical procedure.
